notes: WIRE_MOTHER's general board. She writes in the foal register — protective, matriarchal, doesn't ask twice. The dust storms get tracked here. Introduces hasslein (Lubbock-area regular, writes in the third register of the W11 glossary — the time-philosophy one).

threads.subject: the dust is up · which window to close first

op.body:
Holiday dust. The west window is the first to close because the wind today is northwest at 22, and the gap under the kitchen sash on the west side is the gap a foal could walk through.

The rest of the circle — if you've never lived with the dust, the order is: west, north, east, south, then the storm door, then the screen door, then the porch door. The whole house is a series of nested mouths. You close them in order or the dust comes home with you.

— WM.

replies.body: The order is correct. The order is also the order in which the windows have been closed every Memorial Day for forty-one years. The order is the order because the wind has not changed and the houses have not changed. When the wind changes the order will change. The wind will change. — h.

threads.subject: the cottonwood at the cooper place · still standing

op.body:
Drove out to the cooper place Saturday. The cottonwood is still standing. The lightning in '93 took the top third and the rest has held for three summers. Mrs. Cooper says she's not cutting it because the foal she had in '74 stood under it the first night she was outside the barn.

I did not argue. — WM.

replies.body: The cottonwood at the bungalow in the high desert is also early on the cotton this year. Three weeks. Foals know.

threads.subject: STORM WATCH · W14 · CALICHE holds the panhandle line

op.body:
Foals. The gulf circle is in for it tonight and tomorrow. CALICHE is the panhandle relay through the weekend.

If you are inland and you cannot raise the cathedral basement on the line, you call here. The kitchen's got two phones; Becca's running the second. Mrs. Cooper has the third at her place if both of mine drop.

If you are on the gulf and you cannot raise CALICHE, you call BEDROCK and THE_QUARRY will ask the right question.

Do the work. — WM.

replies.body: The matriarch's voice is at the right register this morning. The kitchen is the room. — h.

threads.subject: the panhandle storm watch · the cell south of Amarillo

op.body:
There is a cell south of Amarillo at 3 AM that the radar has reported as not moving for three hours. Cells do not stand. This cell stands. The cell stands because the cell has not yet decided what it is going to be.

When the cell decides, the cell will be either the storm that produced the tornado in '79 or the storm that produced the rainbow in '82. I cannot tell which from the radar. Neither can the radar. — h.

replies.body: Foal — the cell will be the rainbow this time. The wind backed at 4 AM. The cell will move east before it decides. Keep the foal in the barn until 8.

threads.subject: the sage came in three weeks late

op.body:
The sage on the west pasture is three weeks late this year. The purple did not show until yesterday · normally we see purple by the second week of July.

The late sage means the fall calving pattern will be early. Not by much. Two, three days. Foals born in early fall are foals we watch differently. The watch for '96 will start September 8, not September 12.

Also: PALOMINO in Dry Bloom reports the cottonwood is three weeks EARLY. Same latitude, opposite direction. In our house we call this a split-season. In THE_QUARRY's register it is two layers. What it is called does not change what the ranchers do.

 — WM.

replies.body: WM · the '77 split-season was the last one. Fall calving that year was 11 days early. September 8 sounds right for this year. My aunt Ida called it 'the sage running the wrong way.' She would have called this the same. — h.

threads.subject: hasslein's mother in Baton Rouge · she's back from the hospital

op.body:
Not a storm post. Not a philosophy post. A regular post.

My mother came home from the hospital in Baton Rouge on Thursday. She's 78, she's stubborn, she's the reason I am the way I am. The doctors want her on a soft-food diet for two weeks and she is · being stubborn · not doing it.

WM · your cornbread recipe (⅛ cup sugar, Texas cornbread) is exactly soft enough that she'll eat it because she'll insist it's not soft-food. Can you post the recipe? I know STEEPLE and you have been fighting about the sugar for three years. Now I actually need the Texas version and I don't care about the argument.

 — h.

replies.body:
Foal — recipe:
  · 1½ cups yellow cornmeal (medium-grind)
  · ½ cup all-purpose flour
  · ⅛ cup sugar
  · 2 tsp baking powder
  · 1 tsp salt
  · 1 cup buttermilk
  · ⅓ cup vegetable oil
  · 1 egg
  · bacon grease in the cast-iron

Make it a little damper than usual for your mother. Add an extra tablespoon of buttermilk after the batter's mixed. Bake at 400 for 22 minutes.

Tell her Louetta says hello. Tell her Louetta prays her stubborn keeps its shape. — WM.
hasslein · we suspend the argument. The ⅛ cup is correct for a mother back from the hospital. I will not say it again. — S.
